title: Quiz: Encoding Comparison
description: Test your understanding of encoding method selection

questions.question: Which encoding method uses the fewest qubits to encode N values?

options.text: Basis encoding and amplitude encoding (both log₂N)

questions.explanation: Both basis and amplitude encoding use ⌈log₂(N)⌉ qubits to represent N values. However, amplitude encoding requires O(N) gates for general state preparation, while basis encoding uses O(log N) X gates.

questions.question: For NISQ devices with limited circuit depth, which encoding is typically recommended?
questions.explanation: Angle encoding with shallow circuits is ideal for NISQ devices because it uses O(n) single-qubit rotation gates with minimal depth, making it robust to noise and decoherence.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of data re-uploading?
questions.explanation: Data re-uploading (encoding data multiple times with variational layers between) has been shown to provide universal approximation capability, allowing the quantum circuit to learn arbitrary functions of the input data.

questions.question: Amplitude encoding is always the best choice when you need inner products between data vectors.
questions.explanation: False. While amplitude encoding naturally enables inner product computation via state overlap, the O(N) state preparation cost can negate any quantum advantage. The choice depends on whether efficient state preparation exists for your specific data.

questions.question: When should you consider kernel encoding over variational approaches?

options.text: When you have a very large dataset
When you have a small dataset and O(N²) kernel computation is feasible
When you want to avoid using entanglement
When circuit depth must be minimized

questions.explanation: Kernel methods require computing the N×N kernel matrix, which scales as O(N²). This is feasible for small to medium datasets. For very large datasets, variational approaches that don't require the full kernel matrix may be more practical.
